explanation_md: `p = arr + 1` points to `arr[1]` (value 20). `*(p + 2)` is `arr[3]` (value 40). `p[0]` is `*(p + 0)` which is still `arr[1]` = 20.

prompt_md: Given `int arr[5]{ 1,2,3,4,5 };`, which expression is equivalent to `arr[3]`?
explanation_md: The subscript `arr[i]` is defined as `*(arr + i)`, so `arr[3]` is `*(arr + 3)`. Option b and c both add 3 to the dereferenced value `*arr` (which is 1), giving 4 — a different result entirely.
The pointer walks from `nums[0]` to `nums[2]` (stopping when it equals `nums + 3`), accumulating 5 + 10 + 15 = 30.

prompt_md: Which operation on pointers is undefined behavior?

options.a: Forming the address one past the last element of an array.
options.b: Dereferencing the pointer one past the last element.
options.c: Using `!=` to compare two pointers into the same array.
options.d: Using `++` to advance a pointer to the next element.

explanation_md: Forming `arr + n` (one-past-the-end) is explicitly allowed by the standard and is used as a sentinel. Dereferencing that address — reading or writing through `*(arr + n)` — is undefined behavior because no valid object lives there.

options.a: `p` should be `const int*` to access array elements.
options.b: The offset should be 3, not 0, to reach the last element of a 4-element array.
options.c: `p` must be incremented with `++p` before dereferencing.
options.d: Using `*` on a pointer to an array is invalid.

explanation_md: `p` points to `data[0]`. Adding 0 gives the same address, so `*(p + 0)` is `data[0]` = 7. To reach the last element of a 4-element array, the offset must be 3: `*(p + 3)` = 28.
